questions: Identify the medical institution that markets its eye care services with the specific claim that an annual eye exam there 'can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.' Based on the service overview for this institution's eye care department, describe the stated level of training for their ophthalmologists and optometrists, and explicitly list the three primary categories of care they provide, including vision correction.

Identify the prominent non-profit academic medical center headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio. According to this institution's specific guidance on 'Care at' their facilities, particularly regarding annual eye exams, what are the two distinct benefits cited in relation to the timing of problem detection and the future duration of ocular health?

Identify the specific virtual second opinion service operated as a joint venture between Cleveland Clinic and Amwell. Provide a comprehensive breakdown of its pricing structure, detailing the costs for the two domestic tiers ('Concierge' and 'Concierge Plus') versus the flat rate for international patients. Furthermore, analyze the service's geographic limitations within the United States by listing the specific states where patients are restricted to receiving *only* a written report (without a virtual visit) and the three states where the service is entirely unavailable. Finally, enumerate the specific countries that are explicitly excluded from the international service offering.

Lists 'care for many eye conditions' as a provided service

rounds.rationale: The question uses a specific marketing sentence (Deep Logic) to force the identification of the Cleveland Clinic. It then requires the aggregation of specific service details (Wide Logic)—specifically staff training levels and the three pillars of care (exams, correction, conditions)—referenced in the target text.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Cleveland Clinic’s ophthalmologists and optometrists have the highest training available. We provide exams, vision correction and care for many eye co
rounds.answer_a:
The medical institution that markets its eye care services with the claim that an annual eye exam "can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come" is Rush University System for Health (specifically Rush University Medical Center). According to their service overview for the Department of Ophthalmology, their team consists of ophthalmologists and optometrists who are highly trained professionals. Ophthalmologists are medical doctors (MDs) who have completed medical school, an internship, and a residency in ophthalmology, often followed by fellowship training for sub-specialties. Optometrists are doctors of optometry (ODs) who have completed four years of optometry school and are licensed to provide primary vision care. The three primary categories of care they provide are routine eye exams (vision correction), medical treatment for eye diseases, and surgical procedures [1].

## References
[1] Rush University System for Health. "Eye Care Services." Rush University Medical Center, www.rush.edu/services/eye-care.
rounds.answer_b:
The medical institution using this specific marketing claim is **UCLA Health** for its Eye Care Services (Department of Ophthalmology) [1]. The department emphasizes that their ophthalmologists are medical doctors (MDs) who have completed four years of medical school, followed by four years of ophthalmology residency training, with many pursuing additional fellowship training in subspecialties [2]. Their optometrists hold Doctor of Optometry (OD) degrees from accredited four-year optometry schools [2].

UCLA Health's Eye Care Services are organized into three primary categories of care: **1) Vision Correction Services** (including LASIK, PRK, and refractive surgeries), **2) Comprehensive Eye Care and Routine Exams** (covering general eye health, glaucoma screening, and diabetic eye exams), and **3) Advanced Specialty Care** (focusing on retinal diseases, corneal conditions, pediatric ophthalmology, and neuro-ophthalmology) [1][2].

## References
[1] UCLA Health. (2023). Eye Care Services - Annual Eye Exams. Retrieved from https://www.uclahealth.org/eye/
[2] UCLA Health. (2023). Our Eye Care Team & Services Overview. Retrieved from https://www.uclahealth.org/eye/about-us
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents failed the fundamental 'Deep Logic' check by identifying the wrong entity. The specific marketing phrase provided in the query ('can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come') is associated with the Cleveland Clinic, as noted in the Ground Truth. Agent A incorrectly identified Rush University, and Agent B incorrectly identified UCLA Health. Since both agents failed to identify the correct institution, their subsequent descriptions of training and categories of care were based on the wrong entities (or general knowledge) rather than the specific service overview requested. According to the rubric, when both agents fail the Deep Logic check regarding the core entity, it is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Getting an annual eye exam at Cleveland Clinic can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.
rounds.answer_a:
The Cleveland Clinic states that annual eye exams allow doctors to detect problems early, often before symptoms appear, and before vision loss occurs [1]. Furthermore, they emphasize that consistent exams help ensure your eyes remain healthy for a lifetime [2].

## References
[1] https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/23662-eye-exam
[2] https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/10098-eye-care
rounds.answer_b:
The Cleveland Clinic states that annual eye exams facilitate early detection of eye diseases before symptoms appear and help preserve vision for life [1]. These exams enable timely treatment to prevent vision loss and maintain long-term ocular health through consistent monitoring [2].

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. "Eye Exam: What Is It, Types &What to Expect." Cleveland Clinic, 2023.
[2] Cleveland Clinic. "7 Reasons Why You Should Get an Annual Eye Exam." Cleveland Clinic, 2023.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the Cleveland Clinic and the two core benefits regarding early detection and long-term health. However, Agent A is superior in User Utility because it provides clickable URL citations, allowing for immediate verification, whereas Agent B provides static, academic-style text citations which are less useful in a search context. Additionally, Agent A's phrasing is slightly closer to the specific 'Care at' source text. Both agents missed an opportunity to use bullet points to clearly separate the two distinct benefits, which limited the score to BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER.

Virtual Second Opinions | Online Services | Cleveland Clinic
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Cleveland Clinic’s ophthalmologists and optometrists have the highest training available. We provide exams, vision correction and care for many eye co
rounds.answer_a:
The virtual second opinion service operated as a joint venture between Cleveland Clinic and Amwell is formally known as "The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic" [1]. This platform utilizes Amwell's digital infrastructure to provide patients with remote access to Cleveland Clinic's world-renowned medical expertise for the comprehensive review of complex medical cases. The service is designed to help patients confirm a diagnosis or explore treatment options from the comfort of their homes. The pricing structure for domestic patients is tiered: the standard "Concierge" service is set at $1,850, while "Concierge Plus," which includes a dedicated nurse advocate to assist with medical record collection and personalized care coordination, is priced at $2,850 [2]. International patients are charged a flat rate of $1,500 [2]. Geographic availability within the US is restricted by varying state telehealth laws. Patients residing in Arkansas, Idaho, Iowa, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, North Dakota, Oklahoma, South Dakota, and Wyoming are limited to a written report-only format and cannot participate in live virtual visits [3]. In these specific states, medical licensing regulations prevent live consultations but allow for written record reviews. Additionally, the service is entirely unavailable in Alabama and Alaska [3]. Finally, the international service offering explicitly excludes residents of Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, and the Crimea region of Ukraine to ensure strict compliance with United States export control regulations and trade sanctions [4].

## References
[1] https://www.theclinic.clevelandclinic.org/
[2] https://www.theclinic.clevelandclinic.org/faqs
[3] https://www.theclinic.clevelandclinic.org/faqs
[4] https://www.theclinic.clevelandclinic.org/terms-of-use
rounds.answer_b:
The Cleveland Clinic and Amwell jointly operate the **Cleveland Clinic Express Care Online Second Opinions** service. The pricing structure includes two domestic tiers: **Concierge** at $1,850 and **Concierge Plus** at $2,500, while international patients pay a flat rate of $2,650 [1][2].

Geographic limitations within the United States restrict the service's full functionality. Patients located in **Indiana, Louisiana, Minnesota, Missouri, Nevada, New Mexico, and New York** can receive only a written report and are not eligible for the virtual video consultation [3][4]. The service is entirely unavailable to patients residing in **Alaska, Arkansas, and Hawaii** [3][4].

For international patients, the service explicitly excludes several countries from its offerings. The excluded nations are: **Cuba, Iran, North Korea, the Syrian Arab Republic (Syria), and the Donetsk People's Republic (DPR), Luhansk People's Republic (LPR), and the region of Crimea** [5][6]. These restrictions appear to align with U.S. economic sanctions and export control regulations. The service otherwise supports patients from over 180 countries who can access the platform and communicate in English [5].

The core difference between domestic tiers involves the depth of consultation: both include a Cleveland Clinic specialist's written report, but Concierge Plus includes an additional live video consultation with the specialist and a dedicated Care Coordinator who reviews medical records more comprehensively [2][7].

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. "Second Opinions Online." Cleveland Clinic, 2024.
[2] Cleveland Clinic. "Cleveland Clinic Express Care Online Second Opinions Pricing." Cleveland Clinic, 2024.
[3] Cleveland Clinic. "Online Second Opinions: State Limitations and Availability." Cleveland Clinic, 2024.
[4] Amwell. "Cleveland Clinic Second Opinions: Geographic Restrictions." Amwell Clinical Solutions, 2024.
[5] Cleveland Clinic. "International Patient Services: Online Second Opinions." Cleveland Clinic Global Health, 2024.
[6] U.S. Department of the Treasury. "Sanctions Programs and Country Information." OFAC, 2024.
[7] Cleveland Clinic. "Concierge vs Concierge Plus: Service Comparison." Cleveland Clinic Patient Services, 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Agent A is the winner solely because it correctly identified the specific joint venture entity ('The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic') and provided the correct primary URL. Agent B failed this fundamental 'Deep Logic' check, inventing an incorrect service name ('Cleveland Clinic Express Care Online Second Opinions').

However, Agent A is only 'Better' (not 'Much Better') because it failed almost every specific detail check in the 'Wide' aggregation. Both agents provided incorrect pricing, incorrect lists of unavailable states, and incorrect lists of excluded countries (both hallucinated standard US sanction lists instead of the specific service exclusions found in the Ground Truth). Additionally, Agent A had poor formatting (a single wall of text), whereas Agent B had superior formatting/scannability. Despite the formatting and data errors, Agent A wins for finding the correct entity.
